treatment: Retained title, authors, journal, publication date, DOI, PMCID, PMID, CC BY 4.0 status, and adaptation notice.
reason: Required source identity and license attribution.

treatment: Retained the 17-case count, 15-of-17 cause category, toxicology missingness, and experience missingness. Reframed rarity, correlation, and universal preventability language as unsupported by the design.
reason: High information value only when denominators and overclaim gates remain attached.

source_region: Introduction: BDSM description and research question
treatment: Retained the partnered-activity research question and consent as an inclusion description. Excluded broad prevalence and social-network membership statistics.
reason: Defines review scope without importing unrelated secondary prevalence claims.

treatment: Retained databases, German/English restriction, search-concept families, non-natural-death focus, autoerotic exclusion, and adopted asphyxia classification. Added explicit absent systematic-review elements.
reason: Method is essential for interpreting what the case set can establish.

treatment: Retained high-level distinction among strangulation, suffocation, mechanical asphyxia, and drowning. Did not reproduce the table. Flagged the apparent inversion of traumatic and positional descriptions.
reason: Mechanism language is useful, but the table contains an internal terminology problem and is not a clinical standard.

source_region: Results: publication and German autopsy denominators
treatment: Retained 14 case reports plus one study, 17 compiled cases, 16,437 autopsies, 74 non-natural sexual-activity deaths, and three BDSM-associated cases. Presented the three denominators separately.
reason: Prevents published-case counts from becoming an invented population incidence.

treatment: No individual age/sex combination, relationship, case number, apparatus, body position, act sequence, technique, substance label, or bibliographic case mapping was retained. Only audited aggregate counts were used.
reason: Individual narratives are potentially identifying, sensational, and unsafe as technique reconstructions.

treatment: Used only the fact that age was missing for three cases to illustrate incomplete reporting. Did not use the small selected set to profile participants.
reason: Aggregate demographics have low predictive value without an exposed-population denominator.

treatment: Retained that both participants were described as non-novices in nine cases and that the remaining eight were partial or missing. Excluded individual instruction histories and dangerous preferences.
reason: Supports the bounded lesson that familiarity did not guarantee prevention.

treatment: Relationship categories, meeting history, client status, and observer details were not retained.
reason: The review provides no denominator or supported risk inference from relationship status; details increase identification and sensationalism risk.

source_region: Cause of death subsection and Table 3
treatment: Retained 15 strangulation, one suffocation/oronasal-occlusion, and one hemorrhage-associated category. Excluded every subconfiguration and narrative reconstruction.
reason: The aggregate mechanism signal is safety-relevant; configuration detail is unnecessary and unsafe.

treatment: Retained 13 deceased-person reports, eight positive, five negative, and four same-substance partner reports. Flagged the 64.3-percent denominator as unclear. Excluded named case substances and vague or stigmatizing source labels.
reason: Preserves evidence while preventing detection from being taught as impairment or causation.

source_region: Discussion: diversity of BDSM activities and Table 4
treatment: Did not reproduce the broad practice taxonomy, third-party table, injury list, or instructional discussion. Retained only the source's statement that breath-control risk cannot be eliminated.
reason: Mostly secondary material outside the review's fatal-case evidence and unnecessary for this bounded corpus.

source_region: Discussion: cross-study rarity and incidence comparisons
treatment: Recorded that the paper compares heterogeneous autopsy, autoerotic, natural-death, and publication data, but did not retain those values as comparable rates.
reason: Different definitions and denominators cannot establish relative prevalence or incidence.

treatment: Retained mechanism-versus-slang distinction, uncertainty, scene-change possibilities, witness stress, and stigma as reasons for careful interpretation. Excluded search tactics, device detail, and case reconstruction steps.
reason: Forensic uncertainty is useful; operational reconstruction is outside rope training and could be intrusive or sensational.

treatment: Retained the selected-case co-occurrence and missing-data problem. Explicitly rejected causal or correlational inference without a nonfatal comparison group, consistent assays, and exposure denominator.
reason: The source conclusion overstates what a fatal-only case set can show.

treatment: Retained education, mentoring, communication, emergency planning, rapid-release readiness, and non-eliminable risk as context. Did not reproduce technique instruction or infer safeguard effectiveness. Flagged the source's two-versus-three CPR/instruction count inconsistency.
reason: Safety context is useful only when distinguished from evaluated intervention evidence.

treatment: Professional role prevalence, service descriptions, and training generalizations were not retained.
reason: Secondary, weakly connected to the fatal-case evidence, and prone to stereotype.

treatment: Retained small literature base and stigma/access limitations; added publication, language, ascertainment, denominator, quality-appraisal, and duplicate-report limitations visible from the method.
reason: Limitations determine whether the review can support prevalence or causality claims.

treatment: Retained the published-case neck-compression signal and need for further study. Rejected unqualified rarity, lack-of-knowledge correlation, substance correlation, and universal preventability claims.
reason: Prevents abstract-level overclaims from becoming training facts.

source_region: References, associated data, and article footnotes
treatment: Reference list, external links, article notes, and duplicate data-availability text were not incorporated. Identifiers required for attribution remain in source metadata.
reason: Avoids citation trivia and importing unreviewed third-party claims.

treatment: The authorized CC BY 4.0 JATS is retained for provenance and reproducibility. The snapshot is not a direct-training artifact; only CORPUS.md is training-ready.
reason: Preserves exact source evidence while keeping sensitive narratives and unsafe detail out of the training surface.
